prs.title: [NVFP4][Hopper/AMD Instinct] Add Triton kernels for NVFP4 dequantization and QDQ emulation
prs.author: fxmarty-amd
prs.body_head:
## Purpose

Add Triton kernels for NVFP4 dequantization and quantize-dequantize emulation to accelerate NVFP4 quantization emulation on devices that do not support this dtype natively.

The Triton kernels automatically replace the torch eager reference implementation when running on CUDA devices, with significant perf improvements, and avoids launching many independent kernel when using `--enforce-eager`, with back and forth HBM read/write.

These fused kernels  also help to stop hitting https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/180341 and https://github.com/ROCm/rocm-systems/issues/5071 where many stacked kernels calls from `dequantize_to_dtype` and `ref_nvfp4_quant_dequant` result in HSA crashes in recent ROCm versions / pytorch nightly (`vllm bench serve` with NVFP4 emulation on MI355X is not runnable without `HSA_TOOLS_DISABLE_REGISTER=1` due to these crashes - adding this env var is very slow).


This PR is submitted independently from https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/pull/35737, but closely related to it.

## Test Plan

```python
pytest tests/models/quantization/test_nvfp4.py::test_triton_dequantize_nvfp4 -vvvvv -s 
pytest tests/models/quantization/test_nvfp4.py::test_triton_nvfp4_quant_dequant -vvvvv -s
```

## Test Result

The tests verify:
1. Correctness: Triton kernels produce bitwise-identical results to CPU reference (atol=0, rtol=0)
2. Coverage: Tests multiple tensor shapes (2D/3D), sizes, and global scale values
3. Real weights: Dequantization test uses actual NVFP4 weights from `nvidia/Qwen3-30B-A3B-NVFP4` checkpoint
4. Performance: Benchmarks show speedup vs. CPU reference implementation

## `CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=7 pytest tests/models/quantization/test_nvfp4.py -s -vvvvv -k "test_triton_dequantize_nvfp4"`

```
  dequantize 2D base [512, 1024]:
    triton:    median=0.007ms, min=0.007ms, max=0.008ms
    reference: median=0.088ms, min=0.073ms, max=0.256ms
    speedup:   12.63x
  dequantize 2D 2x rows [1024, 1024]:

prs.title: [ROCm][Perf] Replace WNA16 MoE Triton kernel with FlyDSL MoE
prs.author: amd-asalykov
prs.body_head: ## Purpose This PR replaces WNA16 MoE GPTQ Triton kernel with FlyDSL w4a16 MoE kernel if 1. weights are 4-bit quantized 2. group_size==32 3. arch==gfx950 targeting Kimi K2.5 INT4 model ## Test Plan Measure e2e perf. and test accuracy on gsm8k ## Test Result Accuracy: ~0.94 Perf: ---

prs.title: [ROCm][Perf] Tune GFX11 YTILE/UNRL heuristic for wvSplitK skinny GEMMs
prs.author: mgehre-amd
prs.body_head:
The existing WVSPLIT_TILE_CFG heuristic (YTILE=2 for most N=1 shapes) was suboptimal on GFX11 (RDNA 3/3.5). A parameter sweep over all YTILE and UNRL combinations found that YTILE=1 with appropriate UNRL gives much better ILP for memory-bound decode shapes, while YTILE=4 remains optimal for large shapes where CUs are well-filled.

The new GFX11-specific heuristic (WVSPLIT_TILE macro) also considers K dimension and LDS capacity to pick optimal parameters. The GFX9 fallback in WVSPLIT_TILE preserves the existing heuristic.

bf16 wvSplitK kernel benchmark on Strix Halo (gfx1151, 20 CUs):
  N=1 average: +18.7% bandwidth improvement
  N=2 average: +19.8%, N=3: +15.2%, N=4: +13.0%

fp16 wvSplitK kernel benchmark:
  N=1 average: +6.1% bandwidth improvement

End-to-end benchmark on AMD Strix Halo (gfx1151, LPDDR5X-8000 128 GB), input-len=128, output-len=128, num-prompts=5, Median TPOT (ms):

  Model                     Baseline   This PR    Change
  Qwen/Qwen2.5-0.5B-Instr.    5.18      5.18      0.0%
  Qwen/Qwen3-4B              37.32     36.85     -1.3%   (mean of 3/4 runs)
  Qwen/Qwen3-8B              77.16     70.09     -9.2%
  google/gemma-3-4b-it       38.82     36.63     -5.6%

The 0.5B model shows no measurable chang. Larger models that are decode-bound on the skinny GEMM kernels see significant TPOT improvements.

Reproduce:
  vllm serve <model> --max-model-len 4096 \
    --gpu-memory-utilization <fits-model> --max-num-seqs 1 \
    --no-enable-prefix-caching

  vllm bench serve --model <model> \
    --num-warmups 5 --random-input-len 128 --output-len 128 \
    --num-prompts 5 --max-concurrency 1 --temperature 0 \
    --percentile-metrics ttft,tpot,e2el

prs.title: [Aiter][ROCm] gdn_linear_attn kernel fusion
prs.author: tpopp
prs.body_head:
With more recent aiter kernels, this combines more kernels together, reducing kernel overhead and optimizing further the computations.

For Qwen3Next, the lm_eval accuracy remains consistent at 0.86 and 0.82 for flexible/strict matching and performance improves by 1-4% depending on batch size.

prs.title: [ROCm] Add env flags to disable dynamic MXFP4 quant and enable AITER tuned GEMMs for Attention Projection Layers
prs.author: heachary
prs.body_head:
## Purpose
For models in the DeepSeek family, dynamic MXFP4 quantization is enabled by default for the attention projection layers. This behavior may be suboptimal in certain deployment scenarios.

This PR introduces two opt-in environment variables that provide finer control over this path:

1. The ability to disable dynamic MXFP4 quantization for attention projections.
2. The ability to route unquantized attention GEMMs through AITER's newer FlyDSL-based tuned GEMM implementations, instead of the default skinny-reduce or hipBLASLt paths.

In internal benchmarks, using the FlyDSL GEMM path for unquantized attention projections shows a good performance improvement on models such as Kimi-K2-Thinking.

## Summary
This PR adds two new ROCm environment variables to give operators finer control over quantization and GEMM dispatch for attention linear layers:
- **`VLLM_ROCM_DISABLE_ATTENTION_LINEAR_LAYER_DYNAMIC_MXFP4_QUANT`** (`False` by default) — When enabled, skips the dynamic MXFP4 quantization that is applied to attention projection layers (`q_proj`, `k_proj`, `v_proj`, `o_proj`) which is done upon model load for a certain family of models.
- **`VLLM_ROCM_USE_AITER_TUNED_UNQUANTISED_GEMM`** (`False` by default) — When enabled, routes unquantized GEMMs in the attention path through AITER's tuned GEMM implementation (`aiter.tuned_gemm.tgemm.mm`) instead of the default skinny-reduce or hipBLASLt paths.

Both flags are opt-in and default to the current behavior, so there is no change for existing users.

## Test Result
### Accuracy
Model: Kimi-K2-Thinking-MXFP4, TP=4, MI355X, fp8 KV cache

Benchmark | Metric | Score | Threshold | Status
-- | -- | -- | -- | --
GSM8K (5-shot, 250 samples) | exact_match (flexible-extract) | 0.944 | 0.90 | ✅ PASS
GSM8K (5-shot, 250 samples) | exact_match (strict-match) | 0.936 | 0.90 | ✅ PASS

### Performance
Model: Kimi-K2-Thinking-MXFP4, TP=4, MI355X, fp8 KV cache

| config | conc | Baseline | AiterTunedFI |

prs.title: [Feature] TurboQuant: support hybrid models and uniform quantization
prs.author: JartX
prs.body_head:
# TurboQuant support for hybrid models

This PR fixes TurboQuant startup for hybrid models such as Qwen3.5, Qwen3-Next, and similar architectures.

Previously, TurboQuant would fail with a `NotImplementedError` as soon as it encountered Mamba layers. With this change, hybrid models are now handled correctly: TurboQuant is applied only to `full_attention` layers. 
## Additional fixes

While enabling proper hybrid support, this PR also fixes three additional issues:

- **Page-size planner mismatch:** The hybrid page-size planner was sizing attention pages using the standard formula, which does not match TurboQuant's packed K|V layout. As a result, every TurboQuant attention layer could trigger an assertion in the page merger. The planner now uses the TurboQuant-specific layout.

- **Incorrect backend selection for excluded layers:** If a layer was excluded from TurboQuant — for example because it was a skipped layer, a sliding-window layer, or a Mamba layer — the ROCm/CUDA backend selector could still incorrectly force the `TURBOQUANT` backend. These layers now correctly fall back to the default backend.

- **ROCm `flash_attn_varlen_func` incompatibility:** On ROCm, upstream `flash_attn_varlen_func` does not accept `out=`. A lightweight wrapper now detects that case and copies the result only when needed.

## Summary

Overall, this makes TurboQuant work reliably on hybrid architectures while preserving the current behavior and baselines for dense models.

prs.title: [Feature] Support custom callable proposer backend for speculative decoding
prs.author: CynicDora
prs.body_head:
**Motivation**
Currently, the speculative decoding framework in vLLM primarily relies on loading a complete, separate HuggingFace model as the drafter. However, researchers and systems engineers often need to experiment with custom, lightweight drafting strategies (such as n-gram matching, retrieval-based drafting, or MCTS heuristics) without the overhead of loading an additional full model.

Based on reviewer feedback, this PR integrates a `custom_class` proposer backend into the core engine. Rather than using an arbitrary callable function, it allows users to pass a module path to a custom Proposer class (e.g., `--custom-proposer-backend my_module.MyCustomProposer`). The engine dynamically imports and instantiates this class, natively integrating with its `.propose()` method. This ensures full interface compliance with vLLM's internal proposers while providing a robust, programmable hook for custom speculative decoding logic.

**Modifications**
* Enabled the initialization and routing of `--custom-proposer-backend` within the speculative decoding configuration.
* Implemented `CustomClassProposer` to handle dynamic imports, instantiation, and strict `.propose()` interface enforcement.
* Ensured proper tensor shaping (`[batch_size, draft_len]`) and device synchronization for the outputs generated by the custom class.
* Added a rigorous integration test (`tests/spec_decode/test_custom_proposer.py`) featuring a `DummyDraftProposer` class to validate the end-to-end tensor pipeline and acceptance logic.

**Verification / Test Results**
I have thoroughly verified this integration locally on a single GPU using `facebook/opt-125m`. The engine successfully compiles (with eager mode enforced), hooks into the dummy draft class during the generation loop, and outputs accurate text without any shape mismatches or memory leaks.

<details>
<summary>Click to expand the local integration test logs</summary>

```text
(vllm_github) (base) houyuze@LAPTOP-5F50KIQB:~/v

prs.title: [WIP] Add TRITON_MLA_SPARSE backend for SM80 sparse MLA support
prs.author: haosdent
prs.body_head:
## Purpose

Closes #38006. Enables sparse MLA models (GLM-5, DeepSeek-V3.2) on SM80 (A100/A800) and SM121 (GB10/DGX Spark), where DeepGEMM / FlashMLA-Sparse / FlashInfer-MLA-Sparse are unavailable.

## Changes

1. **Dispatch guard.** `has_deep_gemm()` → `is_deep_gemm_supported()` (SM90+ arch check) in `sparse_attn_indexer.py` and `indexer.py`. Stops DeepGEMM SM90+ kernels from being invoked on SM80/SM121 when the package is installed.
2. **Triton `fp8_mqa_logits` for the indexer.** `mqa_logits_triton.py` reproduces DeepGEMM's prefill + paged MQA logits with a uint8/int bit-trick (`_decode_e4m3fn`) so the kernel compiles on SM80 without `tl.float8e4nv`. Matches the torch reference to bf16 ULPs.
3. **`TRITON_MLA_SPARSE` backend.** `triton_sparse_mla_kernel.py` adds a split-KV decode path with an N-way online-softmax merge, plus a single-pass fast path. Autotune over `(num_warps, num_stages) × (index_topk, NUM_KV_SPLITS, kv_group_num)`; warmed at backend init so no sweep cost in the first request. Masked-out sentinel is `-1e30` (not `-inf`) to avoid `NaN` from `(-inf) − (-inf)` when a whole BLOCK_N tile is masked on short prefills.
4. **Cudagraph support.** `TritonMLASparseMetadataBuilder` (new subclass of the XPU builder) advertises `AttentionCGSupport.UNIFORM_BATCH`; XPU's own builder stays at `NEVER`. On A100 TP=8 this flipped `FULL_AND_PIECEWISE` back on and collapsed the per-layer Python-loop overhead.

Layer 1 of #38006 (the C++ `dsv3_fused_a_gemm` kernel) is already handled upstream — `CMakeLists.txt` builds it for SM90+ only, with a `torch.nn.functional.linear` fallback.

## Benchmarks

Kernel (A30, SM80, 56 SMs):

| shape (nt, topk, H) | XPU kernel | this PR | speedup |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1, 2048, 128 (decode) | 0.297 ms | 0.094 ms | **3.15×** |
| 1, 4096, 128 | 0.580 ms | 0.167 ms | 3.46× |
| 1, 4096, 16 | 0.582 ms | 0.081 ms | 7.17× |
| 128, 2048, 128 (prefill) | 4.912 ms | 4.872 ms | 1.01× |

Decode across all (topk, H): median **4.30×**. Prefill: no regre

prs.title: [Quantization] quantized kv cache loading for models like qwen
prs.author: xuebwang-amd
prs.body_head:
## Purpose This PR aims to fix quantized kv cache model loading issues using AMD-Quark. The fix was initially applied to the `gpt-oss` model and may be extended to other models in `vllm/model_executor/models`, as they share a nearly identical methodology. Here is the original error traceback w/o this PR fixing. ## Here is a check list of some models defined in `vllm/model_executor/models/` for the supportness of quantized kv cache loading. Models with `get_cache_scale` called is marked as ✅, while models without `get_cache_scale` called is marked as ⚠️. The ⚠️ symbol is preferred over ❌ since some models can intrinsically have non-quantized KV cache, for instance: - VL models - MLA models - _etc_ Partial list is: ⚠️baichuan ⚠️bloom ✅ dbrx ⚠️ deepseek ⚠️falcon ⚠️ gemma ✅ gemma2 ✅ gemma3 ✅ gpt_j ⚠️ gpt_neox ⚠️ **_gpt_oss_**: this PR initially aims for ✅ grok1 ✅ hunyuan_v1 ✅ llama ✅ llama4 ✅ mixtral ⚠️ mixtral ✅ phimoe ⚠️ qwen ✅ qwen2 ⚠️ qwen2_moe ⚠️ qwen3_moe ... ## Test Plan ## Test Result ---

prs.title: [ROCm][fusion] enable ROCm rms_norm pattern matching in qk_norm_rope fusion
prs.author: gbyu-amd
prs.body_head:
## Purpose
Current qk_norm_rope fusion can only match the vLLM custom cuda rms_norm. This pr enables AITER custom rms_norm pattern matching for ROCm backends. 

## Test Plan
```bash
#!/bin/bash

export SAFETENSORS_FAST_GPU=1
export VLLM_ROCM_USE_AITER=1
export VLLM_RPC_TIMEOUT=1800000
export VLLM_LOGGING_LEVEL=DEBUG

vllm serve Qwen/Qwen3-0.6B \
    --tensor-parallel-size 1 \
    --max-num-batched-tokens 32768 \
    --trust-remote-code \
    --no-enable-prefix-caching \
    --gpu_memory_utilization 0.9 \
    --compilation-config '{"cudagraph_mode": "FULL_AND_PIECEWISE", "pass_config": {"enable_qk_norm_rope_fusion": 1, "enable_noop": true}, "custom_ops": ["+rms_norm", "+rotary_embedding"]}' \
    --async-scheduling
```

## Test Result
Below log can be observed from the server side:
```bash
(EngineCore_DP0 pid=349086)^[[0;0m DEBUG 11-23 13:46:08 [compilation/qk_norm_rope_fusion.py:235] Fused QK Norm+RoPE on 1 sites
```

Dataset acc with main:
```bash
|Tasks|Version|     Filter     |n-shot|  Metric   |   |Value |   |Stderr|
|-----|------:|----------------|-----:|-----------|---|-----:|---|-----:|
|gsm8k|      3|flexible-extract|     5|exact_match|↑  |0.4185|±  |0.0136|
|     |       |strict-match    |     5|exact_match|↑  |0.4193|±  |0.0136|
```

```
